texts.label: text
texts.orig: Avadhiand Aayush go for a morning walk with their parents. Avadhi notices some shells and tries to pick them up. Her mother advises her not to do so and explains that the shell could be home to a living snail and is actually a part of its body. Avadhi and Aayush wonder how the shell that is not even moving could have a living being inside! Later that day in school, Avadhi and Aayush share this incident with their friends. They approach the teacher to understand how a shell which is not even moving could be a body part of a living snail. The teacher initiates a discussion in the class on living and non-living.
texts.text: Avadhiand Aayush go for a morning walk with their parents. Avadhi notices some shells and tries to pick them up. Her mother advises her not to do so and explains that the shell could be home to a living snail and is actually a part of its body. Avadhi and Aayush wonder how the shell that is not even moving could have a living being inside! Later that day in school, Avadhi and Aayush share this incident with their friends. They approach the teacher to understand how a shell which is not even moving could be a body part of a living snail. The teacher initiates a discussion in the class on living and non-living.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are surrounded by numerous things. Just look around in your classroom and you may find many examples—the pencil that you are holding, the book that you are reading or the pigeon near the window.
texts.text: We are surrounded by numerous things. Just look around in your classroom and you may find many examples—the pencil that you are holding, the book that you are reading or the pigeon near the window.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 10.1 What Sets the Living Apart from the Non-living?
texts.text: 10.1 What Sets the Living Apart from the Non-living?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Look at Table 10.1. Why do you think a pencil is non-living but a pigeon is living? What do you think are the differences between living beings and non-living things according to you? What similarities do the identified living beings share with each other?
texts.text: Look at Table 10.1. Why do you think a pencil is non-living but a pigeon is living? What do you think are the differences between living beings and non-living things according to you? What similarities do the identified living beings share with each other?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have identified movement as one of the similarities among living beings. You have also seen cars moving on a road. Does it mean that a car is living? List the
texts.text: You may have identified movement as one of the similarities among living beings. You have also seen cars moving on a road. Does it mean that a car is living? List the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tasks that you can do but a car cannot. You are a wonderful example of a living being. Whenever you attempt to group things around you as living or non-living, you can compare them with yourself. Which characteristics help you in differentiating yourself from a car? For instance, a car does not grow. Does it mean it is non-living? Now, which characteristics have you used to classify a car as non-living? Continue your discussion in a similar way to identify the essential characteristics of living beings.
texts.text: tasks that you can do but a car cannot. You are a wonderful example of a living being. Whenever you attempt to group things around you as living or non-living, you can compare them with yourself. Which characteristics help you in differentiating yourself from a car? For instance, a car does not grow. Does it mean it is non-living? Now, which characteristics have you used to classify a car as non-living? Continue your discussion in a similar way to identify the essential characteristics of living beings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are some common characteristics that make living beings very different from the non-living things? Let us learn about them.
texts.text: What are some common characteristics that make living beings very different from the non-living things? Let us learn about them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Even though plants do not move from one place to another, they do show certain types of movements. Opening of flowers is one of the examples of movementin plants. Another example of movement in plants is seen in insectivorous plants. Insectivorous
texts.text: Even though plants do not move from one place to another, they do show certain types of movements. Opening of flowers is one of the examples of movementin plants. Another example of movement in plants is seen in insectivorous plants. Insectivorous

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Drosera
texts.text: Drosera

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Drosera is hair-like Whenever
texts.text: Drosera is hair-like Whenever

texts.label: text
texts.orig: plants are dependent on insects for their nutrition. is one of the examples of an insectivorous plant. Drosera featured with saucer-shaped leaves having many projections of unequal length with sticky ends. an insect enters the saucer, hairs move inward and trap the insect with their sticky ends. Try to observe the mechanism of movement in other insectivorous plants. Climbers also wind themselves around any object placed close to them. That means, even though plants do not move from one place to another, they do show some movements.

Try to observe the mechanism of movement in other insectivorous plants. Climbers also wind themselves around any object placed close to them. That means, even though plants do not move from one place to another, they do show some movements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: wear the dress that you used to wear four years ago? No, because you have become larger in size. This is due to growth in your body. Plants and other living beings also grow. Can we consider growth as a characteristic of living beings?
texts.text: wear the dress that you used to wear four years ago? No, because you have become larger in size. This is due to growth in your body. Plants and other living beings also grow. Can we consider growth as a characteristic of living beings?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Living beings need food (nutrition) for their growth and development. List five living beings that require food to grow.
texts.text: Living beings need food (nutrition) for their growth and development. List five living beings that require food to grow.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, think of a process without which we cannot live. Count the number of breaths you take per minute after a normal walk, after a run, and after a few dance steps. Record the data and observe. Do you notice any difference in the number of breaths after each situation? Do you notice the process of breathing in other animals like dogs, cats, cows and buffaloes? Notice the movement of their abdomen while they are taking rest.
texts.text: Now, think of a process without which we cannot live. Count the number of breaths you take per minute after a normal walk, after a run, and after a few dance steps. Record the data and observe. Do you notice any difference in the number of breaths after each situation? Do you notice the process of breathing in other animals like dogs, cats, cows and buffaloes? Notice the movement of their abdomen while they are taking rest.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the process of breathing, when we inhale, the air moves from outside to inside our body. When we breathe out, the air moves from inside our body to outside. Breathing is part of a process called respiration. Do plants also respire? There are tiny pores called stomata on the surface of leaves. These pores help plants in taking air in and out. Interact with senior class students in your school and request if they can demonstrate stomata using a microscope in your class. All living beings respire.
texts.text: In the process of breathing, when we inhale, the air moves from outside to inside our body. When we breathe out, the air moves from inside our body to outside. Breathing is part of a process called respiration. Do plants also respire? There are tiny pores called stomata on the surface of leaves. These pores help plants in taking air in and out. Interact with senior class students in your school and request if they can demonstrate stomata using a microscope in your class. All living beings respire.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you noticed white patches forming on shirts around
texts.text: Have you noticed white patches forming on shirts around

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Water droplets on grass
texts.text: Water droplets on grass

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the armpits during summers? These patches are formed due to sweat. The sweat consists of water and salts removed by the body as waste products. Removal of waste products from the body is called excretion. Urine is also formed as a product of excretion in animals. Do you know that plants also excrete? You may notice plants excrete excess water and minerals in the form of small droplets on leaves. For example, grasses and roses. All living beings excrete.
texts.text: the armpits during summers? These patches are formed due to sweat. The sweat consists of water and salts removed by the body as waste products. Removal of waste products from the body is called excretion. Urine is also formed as a product of excretion in animals. Do you know that plants also excrete? You may notice plants excrete excess water and minerals in the form of small droplets on leaves. For example, grasses and roses. All living beings excrete.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us look at another characteristic. What is your reaction if you unexpectedly step on a sharp object, such as a thorn,
texts.text: Let us look at another characteristic. What is your reaction if you unexpectedly step on a sharp object, such as a thorn,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do plants also respond to stimuli? Yes, plants also respond to stimuli. For example, touchme-not (mimosa, chhui-mui, lajjalu) plants fold their leaves when we touch them. Have you also observed that certain plants fold their leaves after sunset? Specifically, the leaves of certain plants facing each other tend to come together. This can be observed in the sleeping leaves of amla (Indian gooseberry)
texts.text: Do plants also respond to stimuli? Yes, plants also respond to stimuli. For example, touchme-not (mimosa, chhui-mui, lajjalu) plants fold their leaves when we touch them. Have you also observed that certain plants fold their leaves after sunset? Specifically, the leaves of certain plants facing each other tend to come together. This can be observed in the sleeping leaves of amla (Indian gooseberry)

texts.orig: tree. All living beings respond to stimuli. Find a few more plants in your neighbourhood which fold their leaves after sunset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you seen young ones of cats, dogs or other animals? List young ones of five different animals. Have you seen young ones of any non-living things such as a pencil, a chair or an electric bulb?
texts.text: Have you seen young ones of cats, dogs or other animals? List young ones of five different animals. Have you seen young ones of any non-living things such as a pencil, a chair or an electric bulb?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All living beings reproduce. Reproduction is the process of producing new ones of one's own kind. Why is reproduction necessary? It is necessary for the continuity of life.
texts.text: All living beings reproduce. Reproduction is the process of producing new ones of one's own kind. Why is reproduction necessary? It is necessary for the continuity of life.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When a living being is not able to exhibit all of the above mentioned characteristics, despite the availability of all resources (like food, air and water) needed for being alive, it is said to be dead.
texts.text: When a living being is not able to exhibit all of the above mentioned characteristics, despite the availability of all resources (like food, air and water) needed for being alive, it is said to be dead.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In which category would you place a seed—living or nonliving? Why?
texts.text: In which category would you place a seed—living or nonliving? Why?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us explore how a seed germinates to observe some of these essential characteristics in plants.
texts.text: Let us explore how a seed germinates to observe some of these essential characteristics in plants.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: (1). 2 Essential Conditions for Germination of a Seed
texts.text: (1). 2 Essential Conditions for Germination of a Seed

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you observed a seed germinating? You might have wondered what conditions are required for germination of a seed. What conditions do you think are required for seed germination? How will you investigate whether these conditions have an effect on the germination of a seed? Let us find out by performing Activity 10.2.
texts.text: Have you observed a seed germinating? You might have wondered what conditions are required for germination of a seed. What conditions do you think are required for seed germination? How will you investigate whether these conditions have an effect on the germination of a seed? Let us find out by performing Activity 10.2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 10.2: Let.us experiment
texts.text: Activity 10.2: Let.us experiment

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take four identical pots filled with garden soil. Sow four bean seeds in each pot. Now, keep these pots in the following conditions for 15 days.
texts.text: ¢ Take four identical pots filled with garden soil. Sow four bean seeds in each pot. Now, keep these pots in the following conditions for 15 days.

texts.text: = Pot D: Maintain the soil in this pot slightly moist by adding a moderate amount of water on a regular basis. Place this pot in direct sunlight.

texts.text: ¢ Indicate the availability of air, sunlight and water for the seeds in each of these cases in Table 10.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: When a seed turns into a sprout, it is said to have germinated. Predict whether the seeds in each pot will germinate. Record your predictions for each pot kept under different conditions in Table 10.2.
texts.text: When a seed turns into a sprout, it is said to have germinated. Predict whether the seeds in each pot will germinate. Record your predictions for each pot kept under different conditions in Table 10.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Regularly observe the pots for 7-10 days to check the status of germination of the seeds. Record your observations in Table 10.2.
texts.text: Regularly observe the pots for 7-10 days to check the status of germination of the seeds. Record your observations in Table 10.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Compare your predictions with your observations.
texts.text: ¢ Compare your predictions with your observations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you think sunlight is necessary for germination of seeds? Do the seeds in all the pots receive air, water and sunlight? Is there any pot in which air is not available to the seeds? If so, why is it not available? What happens to the seeds in the pot where water is provided in excess? Which seeds receive both air and water? Identify the pots where you can notice the germination of seeds.
texts.text: Do you think sunlight is necessary for germination of seeds? Do the seeds in all the pots receive air, water and sunlight? Is there any pot in which air is not available to the seeds? If so, why is it not available? What happens to the seeds in the pot where water is provided in excess? Which seeds receive both air and water? Identify the pots where you can notice the germination of seeds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do your observations match with your predictions? Write possible reasons in favour of your observations in Table 10.2. Based on your observations, state the conditions which favour seed germination.
texts.text: Do your observations match with your predictions? Write possible reasons in favour of your observations in Table 10.2. Based on your observations, state the conditions which favour seed germination.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Which of the following are essential for seed germination— air, water and sunlight? Compare the available conditions in each pot. Germination of bean seeds requires the right amount of water and air. Why do seeds require these conditions for germination? Do you think that the absence
texts.text: Which of the following are essential for seed germination— air, water and sunlight? Compare the available conditions in each pot. Germination of bean seeds requires the right amount of water and air. Why do seeds require these conditions for germination? Do you think that the absence

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Germinated bean seed
texts.text: Germinated bean seed

texts.label: text
texts.orig: of one or more of these conditions will affect seed germination?
texts.text: of one or more of these conditions will affect : -seed germination?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us understand how these conditions help in seed germination. The effects of the following conditions have been seen in Activity 10.2.
texts.text: Let us understand how these conditions help in seed germination. The effects of the following conditions have been seen in Activity 10.2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water: Seeds require water for germination. Water enables the seeds to carry out the processes necessary for their growth. The outer covering of the seed is called seed coat. Water softens the seed coat and helps the tiny embryo inside it to develop into a plant.
texts.text: Water: Seeds require water for germination. Water enables the seeds to carry out the processes necessary for their growth. The outer covering of the seed is called seed coat. Water softens the seed coat and helps the tiny embryo inside it to develop into a plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Air and Soil: Seeds need air for germination. They use the air available in the spaces between soil particles. Moreover, spaces between the soil particles allow roots to grow easily.
texts.text: Air and Soil: Seeds need air for germination. They use the air available in the spaces between soil particles. Moreover, spaces between the soil particles allow roots to grow easily.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Light and/or dark conditions: We have learnt that for the bean seeds, presence of light is not essential for their germination. In general, most seeds do not require light for germination. But after germination, sunlight is required for further growth of the seedling.
texts.text: Light and/or dark conditions: We have learnt that for the bean seeds, presence of light is not essential for their germination. In general, most seeds do not require light for germination. But after germination, sunlight is required for further growth of the seedling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the Chapter 'Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body', you have learnt that human beings need a balanced diet for good health and proper growth. Similarly, plants too need favourable conditions and nutrients for their proper growth and development. What other conditions do you think would affect seed germination?
texts.text: In the Chapter 'Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body', you have learnt that human beings need a balanced diet for good health and proper growth. Similarly, plants too need favourable conditions and nutrients for their proper growth and development. What other conditions do you think would affect seed germination?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Activity 101, what are the characteristics of living beings which made you place plants in living beings? Do plants show growth in Activity 10.2? Are there any other characteristics of living beings that these plants show?
texts.text: In Activity 101, what are the characteristics of living beings which made you place plants in living beings? Do plants show growth in Activity 10.2? Are there any other characteristics of living beings that these plants show?

texts.orig: How would you now categorise a seed, as living or non-living?
texts.text: How would you now categorise a seed, as living or non-living?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us study another characteristic that can be seen clearly in plants—growth and movement.
texts.text: Let us study another characteristic that can be seen clearly in plants—growth and movement.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 10.3 Growth and Movement in Plants
texts.text: 10.3 Growth and Movement in Plants

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How do plants respond to sunlight? Does sunlight affect the direction of growth of different parts of plants? In which direction would the root and shoot of a plant grow and move if the plant is placed inverted? How would you design an activity to find answers to these questions?
texts.text: How do plants respond to sunlight? Does sunlight affect the direction of growth of different parts of plants? In which direction would the root and shoot of a plant grow and move if the plant is placed inverted? How would you design an activity to find answers to these questions?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 10.3: Let.us design
texts.text: Activity 10.3: Let.us design

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Take some bean or gram seeds and allow them to germinate on a moist cloth or a moist tissue paper.
texts.text: Take some bean or gram seeds and allow them to germinate on a moist cloth or a moist tissue paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Let them germinate until each of them develop into a seedling having a small root and a small shoot.
texts.text: @ Let them germinate until each of them develop into a seedling having a small root and a small shoot.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Now, take three glass beakers or tumblers, and label them as A, B and C.
texts.text: ¢ Now, take three glass beakers or tumblers, and label them as A, B and C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Characteristics
texts.text: Characteristics

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Take three glass plates and attach a thick blotting paper to one side of each plate using a thick soft cotton thread.
texts.text: @ Take three glass plates and attach a thick blotting paper to one side of each plate using a thick soft cotton thread.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Fix one seedling on each plate using a thick soft cotton thread, as shown in Fig. 10.2, ensuring that the plant is not damaged.
texts.text: ¢ Fix one seedling on each plate using a thick soft cotton thread, as shown in Fig. 10.2, ensuring that the plant is not damaged.

texts.text: @ Now, place one glass plate upright with a seedling attached into each of the beaker A and beaker C, as shown in Fig. 10.2a and Fig. 10.2c.

texts.text: Inverted plant kept in sunlight from all directions

texts.orig: Fig. 10.3: Direction of growth of root and shoot under different conditions
texts.text: Fig. 10.3: Direction of growth of root and shoot under different conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the results of this experiment (Table 10.3 and Fig. 10.3), we note that—
texts.text: From the results of this experiment (Table 10.3 and Fig. 10.3), we note that—

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. When the plant is kept upright, the root grows downwards and the shoot grows upwards.
texts.text: When the plant is kept upright, the root grows downwards and the shoot grows upwards.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. When the plant is kept inverted, the root bends and grows downwards. Also, the shoot bends and grows upwards.
texts.text: When the plant is kept inverted, the root bends and grows downwards. Also, the shoot bends and grows upwards.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. When the plant gets sunlight only from one direction, the shoot grows in the direction of light while the root continues to grow downwards.
texts.text: When the plant gets sunlight only from one direction, the shoot grows in the direction of light while the root continues to grow downwards.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After conducting Activity 10.3, we can conclude that shoots of plants grow upward and exhibit movement towards sunlight but roots of plants grow downwards.
texts.text: After conducting Activity 10.3, we can conclude that shoots of plants grow upward and exhibit movement towards sunlight but roots of plants grow downwards.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: _ Know a scientist
texts.text: _ Know a scientist

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jagadish Chandra Bose (1858-1937) was an Indian scientist who did some fascinating experiments with plants. He built a machine called a crescograph to record how plants respond to stimuli like light, heat, electricity and gravity. With this machine, he could measure how fast plants grow. Healso showed that plants can sense and respond to stimuli.
texts.text: Jagadish Chandra Bose (1858-1937) was an Indian scientist who did some fascinating experiments with plants. He built a machine called a crescograph to record how plants respond to stimuli like light, heat, electricity and gravity. With this machine, he could measure how fast plants grow. He also showed that plants can sense and respond to stimuli.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: | ().4 | fe Cycle of a Plant
texts.text: | ().4 | fe Cycle of a Plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have learnt about conditions required for germination and how plants grow and exhibit movement. Let us now explore the changes a plant undergoes in its whole life.
texts.text: We have learnt about conditions required for germination and how plants grow and exhibit movement. Let us now explore the changes a plant undergoes in its whole life.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 10.4: Let us explore
texts.text: Activity 10.4: Let us explore

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Plant a bean seed and provide suitable conditions for its growth. Observe regularly for three months.
texts.text: ¢ Plant a bean seed and provide suitable conditions for its growth. Observe regularly for three months.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Record your observations in Table 10.4 as and when changes become visible.
texts.text: ¢ Record your observations in Table 10.4 as and when changes become visible.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Note the date when any change is observed. Record answers for the following questions—
texts.text: @ Note the date when any change is observed. Record answers for the following questions—

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: How long does it take for any change to occur? Make sketches of various changes that you observe in Table 10.4.
texts.text: How long does it take for any change to occur? Make sketches of various changes that you observe in Table 10.4.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: After how many days does the first flower appear?
texts.text: After how many days does the first flower appear?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: After some parts of the flower have dried, can you see any further growth?
texts.text: After some parts of the flower have dried, can you see any further growth?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Which structure do the remaining parts of flower develop into?
texts.text: Which structure do the remaining parts of flower develop into?

texts.orig: Can you notice a pod or a fruit with seeds develop from a flower?
texts.text: Can you notice a pod or a fruit with seeds develop from a flower?

texts.text: What happens to the plant after the fruits containing seeds are formed?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Go through the observations you recorded regarding the growth of the bean plant in Table 10.4. What changes do you observe after the fruits are formed? Does the plant become yellow and dry even when you continue watering it? Sow the seeds obtained from your bean plant. Watch how the seeds give rise to a new generation of bean plants. Compare the sketches that you have drawn in Table 10.4 with Fig. 10.4.
texts.text: Go through the observations you recorded regarding the growth of the bean plant in Table 10.4. What changes do you observe after the fruits are formed? Does the plant become yellow and dry even when you continue watering it? Sow the seeds obtained from your bean plant. Watch how the seeds give rise to a new generation of bean plants. Compare the sketches that you have drawn in Table 10.4 with Fig. 10.4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A seed grows into a young plant and matures to produce flowers and fruit. The fruit, in this case a pod, contains seeds which give rise to a new generation of bean plants. The entire process from a seed to a plant, and then, to the next generation of seeds is called the life cycle of a plant (Fig. 10.4). When a plant stops growing and all activities of life gradually come to an end, even after the availability of all the necessary conditions, the plant is considered dead.
texts.text: A seed grows into a young plant and matures to produce flowers and fruit. The fruit, in this case a pod, contains seeds which give rise to a new generation of bean plants. The entire process from a seed to a plant, and then, to the next generation of seeds is called the life cycle of a plant (Fig. 10.4). When a plant stops growing and all activities of life gradually come to an end, even after the availability of all the necessary conditions, the plant is considered dead.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 10.4: Life cycle of a bean plant
texts.text: Fig. 10.4: Life cycle of a bean plant

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 ().5 Life Cycle of Animals
texts.text: 4 ().5 Life Cycle of Animals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have learnt about the life cycle of a plant. We have seen that a plant goes through many changes in its life cycle. Have you ever observed how animals grow over time? Draw sketches of their young ones and name them.
texts.text: We have learnt about the life cycle of a plant. We have seen that a plant goes through many changes in its life cycle. Have you ever observed how animals grow over time? Draw sketches of their young ones and name them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 10.5.1 Life cycle of a mosquito
texts.text: 10.5.1 Life cycle of a mosquito

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mosquitoes buzzing around is acommon experience for all of us. Female mosquitoes are bloodsucking insects that transmit several diseases like malaria, dengue and chikungunya. You might have learnt from newspapers, school noticeboards or awareness campaigns that mosquito breeding should be prevented. We are advised not to allow water to stagnate anywhere in our surroundings. Why is it so? Does stagnant water have any relation with mosquitoes laying eggs?
texts.text: Mosquitoes buzzing around is acommon experience for all of us. Female mosquitoes are bloodsucking insects that transmit several diseases like malaria, dengue and chikungunya. You might have learnt from newspapers, school noticeboards or awareness campaigns that mosquito breeding should be prevented. We are advised not to allow water to stagnate anywhere in our surroundings. Why is it so? Does stagnant water have any relation with mosquitoes laying eggs?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conduct a safety audit in your school, or at your home and surroundings to check for stagnant water (if available, carry a hand lens to observe any small creatures). Some common places where stagnant water is likely to gather are desert coolers, planted pots and any open container. You may find two different types of worm-like creatures (Fig. 10.5). They are larva and pupa, two distinct life stages during the development of mosquito. In case you observe larvae and pupae, report to your teacher. Discuss with the teacher and classmates about the necessary measures one can take to prevent breeding of mosquitoes. What differences do you observe in the shape of larvae and pupae?
texts.text: Conduct a safety audit in your school, or at your home and surroundings to check for stagnant water (if available, carry a hand lens to observe any small creatures). Some common places where stagnant water is likely to gather are desert coolers, planted pots and any open container. You may find two different types of worm-like creatures (Fig. 10.5). They are larva and pupa, two distinct life stages during the development of mosquito. In case you observe larvae and pupae, report to your teacher. Discuss with the teacher and classmates about the necessary measures one can take to prevent breeding of mosquitoes. What differences do you observe in the shape of larvae and pupae?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 10.5: Larvae and pupae of mosquitoes in a stagnant water body
texts.text: Fig. 10.5: Larvae and pupae of mosquitoes in a stagnant water body

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mosquito larvae and pupae observed in water bodies repeatedly come to the water surface. What can be the reason for this? Mosquito larvae and pupae live in water and require air to respire. They move to the surface of the water for air.
texts.text: Mosquito larvae and pupae observed in water bodies repeatedly come to the water surface. What can be the reason for this? Mosquito larvae and pupae live in water and require air to respire. They move to the surface of the water for air.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can the life cycle of a mosquito be disrupted?
texts.text: How can the life cycle of a mosquito be disrupted?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I have seen my mother spraying kerosene oil on stagnant water. Why does she do so?
texts.text: I have seen my mother spraying kerosene oil on stagnant water. Why does she do so?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kerosene oil forms a thin layer over the water surface. This layer separates water from air, and does not allow larvae and pupae to inhale air. As a result, they die.
texts.text: Kerosene oil forms a thin layer over the water surface. This layer separates water from air, and does not allow larvae and pupae to inhale air. As a result, they die.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 10.5: Let us analyse
texts.text: Activity 10.5: Let us analyse

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us solve an interesting puzzle.
texts.text: Let us solve an interesting puzzle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How will you decide which stage (larva or pupa) comes immediately after the egg stage?
texts.text: How will you decide which stage (larva or pupa) comes immediately after the egg stage?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suppose you are given a container with water from a puddle containing larvae and pupae. Design an activity to find out the correct sequence of these stages.
texts.text: Suppose you are given a container with water from a puddle containing larvae and pupae. Design an activity to find out the correct sequence of these stages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You can take help of the following activity designed by Avadhi to create your own activity—
texts.text: You can take help of the following activity designed by Avadhi to create your own activity—

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 1: Ihave a water container with mosquito larvae and pupae.
texts.text: Step 1: Ihave a water container with mosquito larvae and pupae.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 2: I will separate 4-5 larvae and pupae into two separate containers with the same water.
texts.text: Step 2: I will separate 4-5 larvae and pupae into two separate containers with the same water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 3: I will observe them every day until I see them changing to the next stage.
texts.text: Step 3: I will observe them every day until I see them changing to the next stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 4: Ifthe larvae change into pupae, it would mean that the larval stage comes before the pupal stage or vice-versa.
texts.text: Step 4: Ifthe larvae change into pupae, it would mean that the larval stage comes before the pupal stage or vice-versa.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 5: I will keep watching both the containers to see in which one a mosquito appears first.
texts.text: Step 5: I will keep watching both the containers to see in which one a mosquito appears first.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These observations will help us to learn the correct sequence of growth.
texts.text: These observations will help us to learn the correct sequence of growth.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now, suppose you are given a container filled with water from a puddle which contains larvae and pupae. Without separating them from the container, how would you design an activity to decide which stage, out of the two, gives way to the next?
texts.text: Now, suppose you are given a container filled with water from a puddle which contains larvae and pupae. Without separating them from the container, how would you design an activity to decide which stage, out of the two, gives way to the next?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 10.6: Life cycle of a mosquito
texts.text: Fig. 10.6: Life cycle of a mosquito

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us learn more about these stages in the life cycle of a mosquito.
texts.text: Let us learn more about these stages in the life cycle of a mosquito.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mosquitoes pass through four stages in their life cycle— egg, larva, pupa and adult (Fig. 10.6).
texts.text: Mosquitoes pass through four stages in their life cycle— egg, larva, pupa and adult (Fig. 10.6).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The adult mosquito that emerges from the pupa rests briefly on the surface of water and then flies away. The adult mosquito may survive for 10 to 15 days.
texts.text: The adult mosquito that emerges from the pupa rests briefly on the surface of water and then flies away. The adult mosquito may survive for 10 to 15 days.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have seen that a mosquito begins its life as an egg (stage I), the egg develops into a larva (stage II), the larva grows into pupa (stage III), and the pupa transforms into an adult mosquito (stage IV). The adult female mosquito lays eggs directly on or near water, and the cycle continues.
texts.text: We have seen that a mosquito begins its life as an egg (stage I), the egg develops into a larva (stage II), the larva grows into pupa (stage III), and the pupa transforms into an adult mosquito (stage IV). The adult female mosquito lays eggs directly on or near water, and the cycle continues.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Significant changes occur in the appearance, body shape and structure during the various stages in the life cycle of a mosquito. The shape of the egg is quite different from the larva; the larva appears very different from the pupa. The pupa appears very distinct from the adult mosquito. Is it easy to imagine that a mosquito emerges from a pupa?
texts.text: Significant changes occur in the appearance, body shape and structure during the various stages in the life cycle of a mosquito. The shape of the egg is quite different from the larva; the larva appears very different from the pupa. The pupa appears very distinct from the adult mosquito. Is it easy to imagine that a mosquito emerges from a pupa?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 10.5.2 Life cycle of a frog
texts.text: 10.5.2 Life cycle of a frog

texts.orig: Activity 10.6: Let us analyse
texts.text: Activity 10.6: Let us analyse

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Avadhi and Aayush are dressed up in full sleeves shirts and full pants today. It has been raining intermittently for a week. They are going out with their classmates for an activity. After a brief walk led by their science teacher, they reach a shallow pond. It is surrounded by trees and tall grasses. The teacher cautions them to watch everything from a distance
texts.text: Avadhi and Aayush are dressed up in full sleeves shirts and full pants today. It has been raining intermittently for a week. They are going out with their classmates for an activity. After a brief walk led by their science teacher, they reach a shallow pond. It is surrounded by trees and tall grasses. The teacher cautions them to watch everything from a distance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: without causing any disturbance. You may also go to a small water body during the rainy season with a facilitator and explore it by taking due safety precautions.
texts.text: without causing any disturbance. You may also go to a small water body during the rainy season with a facilitator and explore it by taking due safety precautions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may notice a white jelly-like substance on the surface of water towards the edge of the pond (Fig. 10.7). This may also be attached to plants growing in or around the water. This jelly-like substance is actually a cluster of eggs of a frog and is known as spawn.
texts.text: You may notice a white jelly-like substance on the surface of water towards the edge of the pond (Fig. 10.7). This may also be attached to plants growing in or around the water. This jelly-like substance is actually a cluster of eggs of a frog and is known as spawn.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observe the features of all the stages of a frog shown in Fig. 10.7. How will you decide the sequence of the given stages (A, B, C, D, E, F)? Some of the stages show distinct changes in their initial and final shapes. Record these changes in Table 10.5.
texts.text: Observe the features of all the stages of a frog shown in Fig. 10.7. How will you decide the sequence of the given stages (A, B, C, D, E, F)? Some of the stages show distinct changes in their initial and final shapes. Record these changes in Table 10.5.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig 10.7: Different stages of a frog ina pond
texts.text: Fig 10.7: Different stages of a frog ina pond

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the observations listed in Table 10.5, draw the life cycle of a frog. Compare the figure drawn by you with Fig. 10.8.
texts.text: Based on the observations listed in Table 10.5, draw the life cycle of a frog. Compare the figure drawn by you with Fig. 10.8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some of the stages have been clubbed together, for example, stages A and F in Fig. 10.7 have been kept under stage I. You will find four stages in the life cycle of a frog— the egg stage, which progresses to the embryo stage; the tadpole stage, consisting of an early stage with a tail and no legs, and a late stage with hind legs; the froglet stage, and the adult frog stage (Fig. 10.8).
texts.text: Some of the stages have been clubbed together, for example, stages A and F in Fig. 10.7 have been kept under stage I. You will find four stages in the life cycle of a frog— the egg stage, which progresses to the embryo stage; the tadpole stage, consisting of an early stage with a tail and no legs, and a late stage with hind legs; the froglet stage, and the adult frog stage (Fig. 10.8).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 10.8: Life cycle of a frog
texts.text: Fig. 10.8: Life cycle of a frog

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Discuss in the class along the following points:
texts.text: Discuss in the class along the following points:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ How are these eggs of a frog different from the other eggs that you may have seen?
texts.text: @ How are these eggs of a frog different from the other eggs that you may have seen?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Which stage has the shortest duration?
texts.text: @ Which stage has the shortest duration?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Is there a change in the habitat during the various stages in the life cycle of a frog?
texts.text: @ Is there a change in the habitat during the various stages in the life cycle of a frog?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ How do the special features support that stage?
texts.text: ¢ How do the special features support that stage?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observe Fig. 10.8. You will see that tadpoles develop legs but still have tails. Tails help them swim in water. Tadpoles grow gradually and start looking like little frogs called froglets. They still live in water but begin to spend some time on land. They continue to grow and lose their tails completely. Their legs become strong to help them jump and land. They become fully developed adult frogs who live both in water and on land.
texts.text: Observe Fig. 10.8. You will see that tadpoles develop legs but still have tails. Tails help them swim in water. Tadpoles grow gradually and start looking like little frogs called froglets. They still live in water but begin to spend some time on land. They continue to grow and lose their tails completely. Their legs become strong to help them jump and land. They become fully developed adult frogs who live both in water and on land.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Plants and animals are a part of the living world. They go through various changes during the course of their lives. We have learnt that a tiny plant grows and develops into a big tree. We have also learnt how animals grow and change from young ones to adults. This journey varies for each animal, making it unique and special. We have seen pupae change into insects, and tadpoles change into frogs. Such changes are important for plants and animals to survive and to maintain continuity of their kind. We should also take care of them and their homes. By nurturing and preserving their homes, we contribute to this flourishing living world.
texts.text: Plants and animals are a part of the living world. They go through various changes during the course of their lives. We have learnt that a tiny plant grows and develops into a big tree. We have also learnt how animals grow and change from young ones to adults. This journey varies for each animal, making it unique and special. We have seen pupae change into insects, and tadpoles change into frogs. Such changes are important for plants and animals to survive and to maintain continuity of their kind. We should also take care of them and their homes. By nurturing and preserving their homes, we contribute to this flourishing living world.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The objects around us can be categorised into two types living and non-living.
texts.text: The objects around us can be categorised into two types living and non-living.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The essential features of living beings are that they move, eat, grow, breathe, excrete, respond to stimuli, reproduce and die. Absence of any of these features indicates that they are not living beings.
texts.text: The essential features of living beings are that they move, eat, grow, breathe, excrete, respond to stimuli, reproduce and die. Absence of any of these features indicates that they are not living beings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Each living being goes through several stages during its life.
texts.text: Each living being goes through several stages during its life.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Germination of seeds depends upon the availability of water, air and suitable light and/or dark conditions.
texts.text: Germination of seeds depends upon the availability of water, air and suitable light and/or dark conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: During germination of seeds, roots generally grow downwards, while shoots grow upwards.
texts.text: During germination of seeds, roots generally grow downwards, while shoots grow upwards.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: A plant's life cycle starts with seed germination, followed by several stages of its growth and development. These include flowering and seed production. Seeds produced during their life cycle would germinate into new plants and the cycle continues.
texts.text: A plant's life cycle starts with seed germination, followed by several stages of its growth and development. These include flowering and seed production. Seeds produced during their life cycle would germinate into new plants and the cycle continues.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The life cycle of an animal as a result of reproduction, begin with a newborn that undergoes various stages of growth and development followed by an adult stage and finally death. The process of reproduction maintains the continuity of its kind.
texts.text: The life cycle of an animal as a result of reproduction, begin with a newborn that undergoes various stages of growth and development followed by an adult stage and finally death. The process of reproduction maintains the continuity of its kind.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Mosquitoes pass through the stages of egg, larva, pupa and adult. The life stages of a frog include eggs, tadpoles, froglets and adults.
texts.text: Mosquitoes pass through the stages of egg, larva, pupa and adult. The life stages of a frog include eggs, tadpoles, froglets and adults.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: In some living beings, such as mosquitoes and frogs, significant changes occur during the various stages of their life cycles. These changes can be seen in body shape, structure and sometimes even in the habitat.
texts.text: In some living beings, such as mosquitoes and frogs, significant changes occur during the various stages of their life cycles. These changes can be seen in body shape, structure and sometimes even in the habitat.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: List the similarities and differences in life cycles of plants and animals.
texts.text: List the similarities and differences in life cycles of plants and animals.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. You have learnt that a tail is present in a tadpole but it disappears as it grows into a frog. What is the advantage of having a tail in the tadpole stage?
texts.text: You have learnt that a tail is present in a tadpole but it disappears as it grows into a frog. What is the advantage of having a tail in the tadpole stage?

texts.orig: 5. Charan says that a wooden log is non-living as it cannot move. Charu counters it by saying that it is living because it is made of wood obtained from trees. Give your arguments in favour or against the two statements given by Charan and Charu.
texts.text: Charan says that a wooden log is non-living as it cannot move. Charu counters it by saying that it is living because it is made of wood obtained from trees. Give your arguments in favour or against the two statements given by Charan and Charu.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Can we consider movementas one of the characteristics to differentiate between the living and the non-living? List five things around you that can move on their own. Do you think that all five things that you have listed can be considered, as living just because they can move on their own? However, unlike animals, plants do not move from one place to another. Do you consider them as living?
texts.text: Can we consider movementas one of the characteristics to differentiate between the living and the non-living? List five things around you that can move on their own. Do you think that all five things that you have listed can be considered as living just because they can move on their own? However, unlike animals, plants do not move from one place to another. Do you consider them as living?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: while walking without shoes, or you accidentally touch a hot cup of tea? Stepping on a thorn and touching a hot object are stimuli. Any thing or any event that prompts living beings to respond is called a stimulus. List three stimuli (plural of stimulus) and your body's instant response to them.
texts.text: while walking without shoes, or you accidentally touch a hot cup of tea? Stepping on a thorn and touching a hot object are stimuli. Any thing or any event that prompts living beings to respond is called a stimulus. List three stimuli (plural of stimulus) and your body's instant response to them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some seeds of flowering plants, like Coleus and Petunia, require light to germinate. Covering these seeds with soil inhibits their sprouting. Seeds of flowering plants, like Calendula and Zinnia, need darkness to germinate. These seeds . : : should be covered with sufficient soil.
texts.text: Some seeds of flowering plants, like Coleus and Petunia, require light to germinate. Covering these seeds with soil inhibits their sprouting. Seeds of flowering plants, like Calendula and Zinnia, need darkness to germinate. These seeds . : : should be covered with sufficient soil.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The silk moth also passes through four life stages—egg, larva, pupa and adult. Eggs hatch into larvae, which then grow in size. Larvae secrete thread-like material which they wrap around themselves, before changing to pupae. These are the fibres that are used to make silk fabric. ® In India, the Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC) has set up several centres for silk production. Do
texts.text: The silk moth also passes through four life stages—egg, larva, pupa and adult. Eggs hatch into larvae, which then grow in size.
